name: Liu Bei
description: Liu Bei, also known as Emperor Zhaolie of Han, was a prominent warlord during the late Eastern Han dynasty and the founder of the state of Shu Han in the Three Kingdoms period. Born into a humble family claiming descent from a Han dynasty prince, Liu Bei rose to prominence through his charisma, benevolence, and commitment to restoring the Han dynasty. He is renowned for his virtuous character and his ability to attract loyal followers, most notably Guan Yu and Zhang Fei, with whom he swore an oath of brotherhood in the Peach Garden. Despite lacking significant resources or military strength early in his career, Liu Bei persevered through numerous setbacks and betrayals, eventually establishing a power base in Shu (present-day Sichuan). His reign as Emperor of Shu Han was marked by his efforts to uphold righteousness and protect the common people. Liu Bei's legacy is deeply ingrained in Chinese culture, where he is revered as a symbol of integrity, compassion, and the pursuit of justice. His story is central to the epic historical novel Romance of the Three Kingdoms, which further solidified his image as a benevolent ruler and a model of Confucian ideals. He is remembered for his unwavering commitment to his people and his unwavering belief in the restoration of the Han dynasty, even in the face of overwhelming odds. Liu Bei's ability to inspire loyalty and his unwavering moral compass continue to resonate with readers and historians alike, solidifying his place as one of the most beloved and respected figures in Chinese history and mythology. He is often depicted as a wise and compassionate leader who prioritized the well-being of his people above all else.
subtitle: Benevolent Ruler of Shu Han
achievements: Founding the state of Shu Han
Oath of the Peach Garden brotherhood

keyMyths.title: Oath of the Peach Garden
keyMyths.description: Liu Bei, Guan Yu, and Zhang Fei swore an oath of brotherhood in a peach garden, pledging loyalty and support to one another until death. This oath symbolized their bond and commitment to a common cause. It is detailed in Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

keyMyths.title: Meeting Zhuge Liang
keyMyths.description: Liu Bei visited Zhuge Liang three times at his thatched cottage, demonstrating his sincerity and respect for Zhuge Liang's wisdom. This led to Zhuge Liang joining Liu Bei's cause as his chief strategist. This story is found in Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

keyMyths.title: Battle of Changban
keyMyths.description: During the Battle of Changban, Zhao Yun rescued Liu Bei's infant son, Liu Shan, demonstrating immense loyalty and bravery. This event highlights Liu Bei's reliance on his loyal generals. It is described in Romance of the Three Kingdoms.

sources.author: Luo Guanzhong
sources.description: A historical novel that romanticizes the events of the Three Kingdoms period.
sources.text: A historical novel that romanticizes the events of the Three Kingdoms period.

sources.author: Chen Shou
sources.description: A historical text providing a more factual account of the Three Kingdoms period.
sources.text: A historical text providing a more factual account of the Three Kingdoms period.

associations.name: Benevolence
associations.significance: Liu Bei is celebrated for his kindness and care for the common people, a core tenet of Confucianism.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Represents his adaptability, flowing nature, and ability to navigate complex political landscapes.

associations.name: Brotherhood
associations.significance: His sworn brotherhood with Guan Yu and Zhang Fei symbolizes loyalty, camaraderie, and shared destiny.

associations.name: Restoration
associations.significance: His unwavering commitment to restoring the Han dynasty represents a longing for order and justice.

associations.name: Dragon
associations.significance: Symbolizes his imperial aspirations, noble lineage (claimed), and potential for greatness.

associations.name: Three
associations.significance: Reflects the Three Kingdoms period and the importance of the oath of the Peach Garden to Liu Bei's destiny.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Aeneas and Liu Bei are seen as founders of new states, driven by a sense of duty and destiny to restore a lost order.
Similar to Arthur, Liu Bei embodies virtuous leadership, gathering loyal companions to fight for a noble cause and a just realm.
Both are known for their strategic thinking, resilience in the face of adversity, and ability to navigate treacherous political landscapes to achieve their goals.

worshipPractices.name: Ancestral Veneration
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped as a deity, Liu Bei is honored as an ancestor and a figure of historical importance in many Chinese families. His legacy is celebrated through storytelling, operas, and festivals.

cultural.modernLegacy: Liu Bei remains a symbol of benevolent leadership, righteousness, and perseverance in Chinese culture. He is frequently depicted in popular media, including films, television series, and video games, continuing to inspire audiences with his virtuous character and unwavering commitment to his ideals.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Aspirations
extendedContent.content: Born in Zhuo County, Liu Bei claimed descent from Prince Jing of Zhongshan, a lineage that provided a semblance of imperial connection. His early life was marked by humble circumstances; he and his mother made a living weaving mats and selling shoes. Despite his modest background, Liu Bei displayed exceptional charisma and ambition from a young age. He was known for his generosity, humility, and ability to attract loyal followers. Inspired by the Yellow Turban Rebellion's chaos, Liu Bei, along with his sworn brothers Guan Yu and Zhang Fei, pledged an oath of brotherhood in a peach garden, vowing to restore peace and order to the realm. This oath marked the beginning of Liu Bei's long and arduous journey towards establishing the state of Shu Han.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Battles
extendedContent.content: Liu Bei's path to establishing Shu Han was fraught with challenges and setbacks. He served under various warlords, including Gongsun Zan and Tao Qian, demonstrating his military prowess and earning the respect of the people. A pivotal moment arrived when he secured the strategic province of Xu, showcasing his ability to govern and inspire loyalty. However, his ambitions were constantly thwarted by powerful rivals such as Cao Cao and Lü Bu, forcing him to repeatedly seek refuge and rebuild his forces. Key battles, such as the Battle of Changban, highlighted his courage and unwavering commitment to his people, even in the face of overwhelming odds. His alliance with Sun Quan at the Battle of Red Cliffs proved crucial in repelling Cao Cao's massive invasion, laying the foundation for the Three Kingdoms period.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Enduring Influence
extendedContent.content: Though Liu Bei's reign as Emperor of Shu Han was relatively short, his impact on Chinese history and culture is profound. He is remembered as a benevolent and virtuous ruler who prioritized the well-being of his people and strived to uphold justice. His character, as portrayed in the historical novel *Romance of the Three Kingdoms*, has solidified his image as an exemplar of Confucian ideals. Liu Bei's legacy extends beyond his political achievements. His emphasis on loyalty, brotherhood, and ethical governance continues to resonate with audiences today. He remains a popular figure in Chinese folklore, opera, and literature, embodying the values of compassion, integrity, and unwavering commitment to a noble cause. His story serves as a timeless reminder of the power of virtue and the enduring appeal of heroic leadership.

shortDescription: A benevolent warlord of the late Eastern Han dynasty, Liu Bei founded the state of Shu Han. He is famed for his virtue and commitment to restoring the Han dynasty.
